title: Andrew Reese -Executive Interview | Founding Account Executive
meeting_title: Andrew Reese -Executive Interview | Founding Account Executive

transcript.text: Hello, sir.
Good to see you again.
How's it going?
You again, too.
Going pretty well.
How was your fourth?
It was good, but it was kind of busy, so I needed a holiday for my holiday kind of thing.
I know how that goes.
I was in Durango, Colorado for a wedding, and it was fun, but I feel the same way.
I'm just exhausted.
We got back at like 2 last night.
Good.
So you're probably running on fumes.
Yeah, exactly.
Hard place to travel to.
It's a small town.
It's funny, the bartender at the wedding was also the mayor of Durango.
Stop it.
Yeah.
That is it.
That is
Wow, that is next level small town stuff, you know.
Yeah.
It's like, I'm the mayor and I bartend at weddings part-time and I Uber the guests afterwards, you know.
Exactly, it's like the one Uber in town.
And it was so weird.
kind of, it was a fun wedding, but it's also a little bit awkward because everybody, such a small town that it's like all the people that are eligible to have dated each other.
They all had, like, everybody there had, like, dated each other at some point.
Like, the bride, all of her friends there, it's like ex-boyfriends, just because it's such a small town that, that is the pool, like, the people your age.
I bet, like, yeah, that is awkwardly interesting, you know.
It was, it was definitely interesting.
That's funny.
How are you connected to that?
Were you just, like, know someone that has, like, moved there or did you group?
Yeah, my wife, one of her good friends.
Yeah.
She lived here in Austin, you know, became good friends.
And then she, I guess it was during COVID, she bought a house up in Durango, and she just ended up really liking it there.
So she moved up there and met one of, like, four dudes that was, you know, single in her age group there.
Yeah, you got a 25% chance.
Yeah, exactly.
That's awesome.
Well, how are you feeling overall?

Well, like, I always like to start with, like, any questions and things that you haven't had a chance to talk through, you know, before we dive in a little bit, but happy to kind of answer questions before talking about other things.
Yeah, appreciate it.
I mean, I really, really enjoyed the process.
The team seems great.
Everybody's been just, everybody seems like someone, at least from what I could tell, and I feel like they really are just people that I would enjoy working with.
And, you know, already it's easy to get along with the team.

So I've really enjoyed the people, learning about the process, you know, kind of the product, really the approach, especially coming from a similar model where we're like very much a bespoke AI implementation, how we work, how we implemented our product at SOLDA.
Yeah.
Yeah, I don't really think I have any big questions top of mind now.
I mean, I guess one question is, so you're $7 million in revenue, is that right?
Yeah, a little over that in run rate.
Nice.
And what the average contract value is, I think it's, you used to start around $9,000 a month, and now it's $12,000, is that right?
Yeah, of our recent deals have been like $15 plus, but I think there's also like this fine line of, like, as we open up more of the platform.
And the platform becomes easier, better, faster.
And then there is a path towards these kind of strategy sprints becoming just kind of like a setup fee, if you will.
And then there are opportunities to do platform-only deals.
And that I think can start to look very different.

It's just right now, it doesn't matter what you build, if you just unleash people into a platform, they're just not going to be successful because their teams are all busy and don't know what they're doing anyways.
And so it's not about a technology problem.
It's more of a forcing the right inputs out of customers in order for them to get value of anything, like whether it's a service or a platform, like it doesn't matter.
You know, they just don't have clarity on a lot of what they need.
Like you go to a company and you say like, hey, can you send me a description of your company?
Non-existent.
Can you send me a description of your product?
Non-existent.
Hey, who are your personas, like audiences that you care about?
Non-existent.
Hey, oh yeah, we had this thing from...
Years ago, super outdated, but here you go, right?
And, okay, what do you want to accomplish?
You know, just want to grow.
Okay, cool.
Like, you know, it's like people don't have, like, really good things formulated, so you have no good inputs to use.
So unless, like, you actually go and spend hours and hours and say, hey, can we talk to your head of product?
Can we talk to this?
And then go interview them and do that in the first couple of weeks.
It's not a lot of work.
It's just, like, doing, like, four hours worth of deep dives.
Then you have, like, amazing inputs and artifacts that then you can use for everything else.
And then, like, you align on expectations.
But if you don't do that, it's just, like, man, it's just, you know, people just think, like, yeah, just come up with a bunch of keywords, play, and call it a day, right?
Like, that's it.
And so that's kind of, like, a little bit of the gap right now.
And so if we went and try to do, like, we try to play around with, like, $6,000 deals.
We even did, like, $4,000 a month one, and it's the exact same amount of work.
Like...
And the expectations are actually higher because they really don't know their .
And so they expect you to do way more work, actually.
So it's almost like a $6,000 a month requires more work, more hand-holding, and they're more likely to not pay than normal security at $25,000 a month.
Yeah, that makes a lot of sense.
for the insight.
That's very similar to Miracle and Canva, and actually Solda, for that matter.
It's like the ones that have less resources to invest up front have less work done and less resources that they have invested into everything before that, too.
So smaller the contract, smaller the customer, more work.
That makes sense.

And I guess, yeah, it's interesting because it seems like any of the jobs-to-be-done theory has never been more true in this space, where people with AI, they really are trying to put that quarter-inch hole, not the quarter-inch drill.
And with AI, it's like...
You know, it's not even just, like, you know, no one knows how to use the drill.
Like, they have to assemble the drill before they could even use the bit.
And so it's, you know, it's really interesting just seeing, like, working at Solda talking to customers and just seeing how little people know about AI.
And, like you're saying, all that pre-work, all the inputs that go on, like, they don't even have the inputs to even get to even remotely close to the outcomes they want.

So, yeah, I really like the approach of, like, you know, service as a software because people just, AI is still, like, buy revenue for most AI companies right now because people like the idea of trying it out, but they're not getting, it's like a garbage in, garbage out type.
Yeah.
And so, yeah, I mean, I think it's a really cool model.
Yeah, yeah.
Well, I appreciate it.
And I guess, like, as you think about kind of this role, right, I'm always really curious, what do you feel like?
It's truly like your superpower, where along your career, you were substantially better than most of your peers at certain things.
Like, what do you think that is for you?
Yeah, that's a good question.
I'd say, first off, like, the area is where I'm not the strongest.
I think as I, just as a salesperson, I'm okay.

I think my strengths as a salesperson is I'm very customer-centric, and I like problem-solving, and I like building relationships, but there's a salespeople that are more polished and, you know, just more, you know, smooth than I am.
But I think where I stand out is just always being customer-centric, but then also stepping back beyond sales.
There's, you know, both salespeople just can put blinders on.
They only focus on sales, and they don't really like wearing other hats.
And I always, I like taking just extreme ownership of any role that I step

And, you know, you got to take a step back and for sales to be successful, be able to scale any kind of sales process, you need to look at just the general go-to-market foundation, like understanding your ICPs and personas and your messaging and positioning and all these other aspects, these foundational things that I think I have a unique blend of understanding how to do both.
And then taking a customer-centric approach to building and scaling a sales process.
That makes sense.

Okay, so it's very, like, first principles, process-oriented, caring about the customers so that, like, you have kind of built that empathy where they feel like they're collaborating with you versus, like, you selling to them.
Is that, like, a fair way to play?
Exactly.
Like, helping them solve their problems and being a trusted advisor or a consultant in the process.
Like, just becoming an expert.

On the product that I'm selling, and the market that I'm selling to, and the personas, and just being able to say, like, hey, first off, know them well enough to understand their workflows well enough, and their profile, and, like, just all the insights that I gather through other conversations to know what kind of questions to ask, to uncover the right pain points, and then know how to help guide them towards that aha moment, and have, like, a, enough of an understanding to come up with a good point of view that they would hire a McKinsey or VCG consultant to figure out for them.
Cool.

And so, like, if you think, let's call it, like, last two years, so that would affect, like, pre-COVID or anything, or, you know, like, I know there's been, like, a lot of weird waves and things like that.
Yeah.
What's a deal that you managed from, kind of, like, A to Z that you felt like, if all my deals were like this, I would be in heaven, you know, like, walk me through maybe one of
And then we'll get to the nasty ones, you know, but like...
I got plenty of nasty ones, too.
So a good one, the last two years.
So at Canva, there was, I closed, it was the biggest deal of Q2 globally, and the fifth biggest deal of all time last year.
And it was with Edward Jones, you know, the financial services company.
And I spent over a year working on that deal.
It was quite a process.

And my approach, I always try to talk to, when it makes sense, you know, depending on the product I'm selling, talk to practitioners first, the people that are living the pain every day, and work around that way, I guess, start just talking to anybody that would talk to me and understanding them.
And I like the approach of understanding the current state and their work flows end to end, the challenges they're facing, the negative consequences that are...

That's leading to, and then understanding what their ideal state is, know, if they could just, you know, snap a finger and address it, what that looks like, and what kind of goals that those are kind of packaged into, and then understanding if there's any initiatives or projects going on to address those, like, formal progress, working towards those, and then trying to attach, you know, that map the difference between where they are today and those challenges to where they want to be, map what we can do to bridging that.
And then attaching to that project to, like, kind of drive the conversation forward, and so with Edward Jones, I talked to over 20 of these branch office administrators.

I found that there's 65,000 employees there when I was researching the company, and 20,000 of them are, are, just had the same title, branch office administrator, and so Canva realized that, you know, looking at their job descriptions, they, their job you.

Christian was almost exactly what, you know, they're responsible for the things that Canva could provide, that we needed to create follow-up assets for, like, all their client meetings, for the financial advisors, and send out marketing materials, and just basically creating brand content all the time.

And so I started reaching out to them with really tailored outreach, like, hey, I understand that you're responsible for these things, I'd love to chat, learn a bit more, and see if we might be able to help you out.

And so I started talking to a few, and then after some good conversations, they started referring me to, you know, their peers, and then eventually started referring me up the chain to the branch office leadership.

And the whole time, I'm just trying to collect as much insight as I can, doing rediscovery with all these new branch office admins, and just collecting these insights and kind of distilling them so I can frame my point of view.
And then by the time I got to the regional branch office director, which is one of the first leaders I talked to.

I had a really strong point of view and said, hey, sounds like these dozen or so people I've talked to, here's what they do, and here's their challenges day-to-day and the gaps in their process, and here's how we can fix it and kind of where we map in and then the positive business outcomes you'd see.

And so I developed this guy into a champion, and he ended up bringing his counterparts in different regions, and then kind of all banded together to get marketing because they're a franchise company or an agency model.

So they have all the agencies, and then they have the corporate division, and so they all banded together, and we kind of worked together to just bug corporate marketing enough to the point that they took a meeting.

And so with them, I was able to recap everything, just kind of come in like a consultant and think, hey, here's the current state, understood it as well as them, at least in some ways, and so I was able to position it like, here's what's going on.
And I also did some surveys just to gather insights and direct snippets from the branch office admins.
And so I pitched this whole vision, current state, ideal state, and here's where we help bring you through that gap.
And so we formalized a pilot.

It was a three-month pilot, and during that time, I really got laser-focused on building relationships with everybody, making sure that I had strong relationships with the corporate marketing team, the PMO person that was involved, understood all their roles and what they cared about, and then relined on what they considered success, like what success looked like to them and those metrics that they're measuring it by.
And so we just really built, designed that whole pilot to make sure that they're seeing those success metrics that they needed to see.
And then I kind of paired off with, you know, different conversations with different groups that care.

About different elements of it, and then stayed in close contact with them throughout it, you know, having weekly or bi-weekly syncs with different groups, just so they felt like, you know, they could really lean on me in building that partnership there.
And at the end of the pilot, there was overwhelming support for it, because we were so aligned with the problems that the end users were facing, that they really helped build a strong case.
And we had all the data and all the survey insights we needed to really quantify the impact.
And then we ended up signing a $250,000 deal to start with a plan of, six months later, growing to a million in ARR.
Nice, nice, that's awesome.
And, like, was the pilot paid?
Yes, the pilot was $15,000.
Okay, for like three months of work, essentially.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
And then the pilot was, like, for different, like, offices or whatever, or a set number of offices, or was it to just, like...
What were you trying to validate in the pilot?
We had a group of 300 people to test with for the pilot.

They're a mixture of people that had signed up for Canva before that I was able to find and provide them a list, like here's people that are already using Canva, and then some that they had gotten requests for, where people were requesting tools for this.

So anybody that had ever reached out to corporate marketing requesting some kind of, you know, saying there's a gap in the system, it was a combo of that, and they were spread across a bunch of different branches.
Yeah, that makes sense.

And so, like, on the flip side of that, right, is there a good example of where a deal almost like kind of fell stuck, if you will, right, like somebody, like you put a lot of work or something, or they seem to have like a lot of high...

And then all of a sudden, you know, they're either ghosting or it just kind of went cold and you were able to do something to kind of bring them back to the table or it got stuck somewhere and you did something that helped unstuck, you know?
Yeah.
Do you want one that I won or that I lost?
Both, yeah.
So, with Nubank at Miro, they were the biggest, one of the biggest deals we've ever closed at Miro is 1.4 million AR, or total contract value of a two-year contract, so 700K a year.

And I started working with them back in 2020, I think it's 2020, and they, they basically, they're growing really quickly, 70% year over year, they're blowing up, and they're hiring people all over the world, COVID really just threw a wrench in things because
They're already just growing quickly and growing a global footprint, but they now couldn't meet up.
They couldn't travel and have these onsites.
So they're having this huge bottleneck in collaboration.
everybody had different tools that they used because everybody was bringing the tools that they used at their previous companies.
They didn't have a directive IT or PMO strategy for the tools that they used.
And so knowledge was just scattered across different tools.
People weren't really, there was a lot of opportunities for efficiencies there.
And people just were living in different tools, storing information in different tools, or just not, didn't know how to best collaborate with others.
So I started working with our head of product for an initial 50 license deal.
And we, you know, did well there.
Like we really focus.
Like.

How I was just saying with Edward Jones, took that same approach to really understanding where they were, where they're trying to go, and then really hand-holding them through that process of helping them roll out, like, the use cases in Miro that helped move the needle the most.
And after getting, you know, we got some good traction there, and she became a champion to bring in other product teams.

So I started looking at lookalike teams, so other product teams, and then adjacent teams, so not working with product, but also design and engineering, and just started trying to expand through the org as much as I could.
And so fast forward a bit, you know, I've got, it's now, there's 50 users, and it's grown to, you was like 500 or 750 or so.
And at that time, you know, we started working towards an ELA, so we upgraded, we got them to $172,000 contract, and I was trying to figure out how to just push them.

So I made them a deal with that renewal that I'd give them an unlimited licensing for the next year without any true-up costs, and I worked with them to help make sure that they're seeing the value that they want, but in exchange, I need to get access to all the leadership across the different lines of business, product, engineering, and design, and marketing as well.
And then also just getting the access I need, and the support, and basically the attention from their side to make sure that we could really make this successful.
So I spent the next year connecting with tons of people.
think I had over 70 meetings with different people and different teams at the new bank, and I got really good traction there.
But when it came up for negotiation for the renewal, we had grown a lot at Miro, and we hired a lot of new.

Sales and customer success leadership, and since it was, you know, one of the biggest deals that we've ever done, of course, you know, understandably, everybody in leadership wanted to get involved in it and have their name on the deal.

So all these people that had just started, just hopped in, and they had the best intentions, but I think one of my learnings is I should have managed up better and managed internally better, because we had all these people come in, the customer, they're Brazilian, and, you know, Brazilian culture is very relationship-focused, as you probably know, and it's just much more, you've got to be much more, I guess, can't be too American on these sales calls.
So, our head of sales, who is very, very, you know, very much American-style sales, he came in and
It was this call with the head of procurement, head of IT, and then a couple other people who I had great relationships with.
And so we joined.
They wanted a small talk a little bit.
And he just jumped.
He's like, let's just get straight into it.
And he started interrupting them and doing this whole strong arm thing.
Like, you can either take it or leave it kind of thing.
And he's the one that came up with this number that he wanted to try to get $2 million.
And, you know, that wasn't going to be possible based on my previous discussions.
And so he just kind of came in over the top, said, this is it, take it or leave it.
They got really upset because they just felt like the relationship, it was a bait and switch.
You we just pulled the rug out from under them.
And so they kind of pulled back afterwards.
I got, you know, this email like, hey, this isn't the relationship that we've had.
This isn't, you know, this isn't going to work for us.
And so I spent time with, you know, getting on calls with my team, trying to iron it over and just kind of falling on my own sword, saying, hey, sorry, I shouldn't.

I should have managed better internally, just give me some time, I'll go talk with the team, make sure that we're aligned internally on what we discussed previously, and make sure that we're working towards the vision that we've discussed over this last year, and so I went internally, and after, you know, that fail, that call, our head of sales was more receptive to taking another approach, so I pitched, here's what I understand.

Like, I basically built an internal business case, or like, let's offer them 1.4 from all these conversations with their procurement team and IT team understanding what their requirements are for commercial terms, like, they wanted to have the per license cost under a certain amount, and that was the main thing for them, and they wanted it to be, you roughly in the same range of their Microsoft spend, so we got really creative with the terms, and were able to get them, you know, that 1.4 million dollar proposal, I put it in front of them, and and,
We were back on, you know, they felt like this is back in the direction that we were talking, and it ended up resulting in that deal.
Yeah, that's awesome, man.
Thanks, man.
Well, that's super, super helpful.
Like, one last quick one, and then, like, because I got to run in a couple minutes, and then I'm happy to answer any quick questions.
But, like, how do you think about, I love the resourcefulness and the relationship building in a lot of these deals.
How do you think about, kind of, where we are, which is kind of weird, because, you know, we're closing, like, last week we closed the $720,000 deal, and it was three days, you know.

It was, like, with Surge.ai, and, you know, and obviously it's, we can still opt out within the first 60 days, and, you know, the relationship is strong, and, you know, right now we're working on a few others where it's just, like, you know, going from, like, nine a month to 30 a month or something, right?
But for most cases, it's, like, our good deals will close in less than a month.
Or, you know, close to 200 a year, right?
Like, and so, yeah, how do you adapt that to that, like, essentially, like, if we took a year for 2020, it would be really bad.
And I know that's completely different.
So there's, like, two sides for me.
Like, one is, how do we adapt to that?
Like, it feels, like, high momentum, right?
And it's, like, it's a different kind of, like, motion a little bit.
still need to do the relationship building, but it's almost like you need to allocate time for the relationship building.
But then you just need to, like, have really good shots on goal and just, like, create urgency and get people, like, in or out.
Like, you've to sign up.
Otherwise, you're, like, not starting for another two, three months, you know, kind of thing.
And the other side of that is, like, there's no usage, Like, there's no, like, hey, you're using this.
You see the value.
Like, let's go talk to your team.
So it's, I think, less climbing the ladder, you know, in some ways, potentially.
So how do you, like, adapt to that, potentially, you know?
Yeah, that's a good question.
And I think, well, that sounds like every salesperson's dream, you know, come in.
Like the scale cycle for six-figure deals, and so I think the difference is at Miro, we had to prove ourselves.
Miro, Canva, and Solda, we had to prove ourselves before.

We're selling them the tools for the inputs, and we had to sell them and train them and kind of get them to understand how to use it on their own to see the inputs, but when you're selling outcomes, I think the shift would be like we need to just get them into that 60-day opt-out.

Just move towards that, and so I think, you know, similar to, you know, after the mock call, how you said, it's just focusing, like we have, there's so many great proof points out there, just making sure that they understand what's, I think it's a lot more linear, you you just explain, like here's what we're doing for others, and here's our process, and here's what it looks like from now on, and like just get them into the contract, and then the growth strategist would do a lot of that, that work that would kind of nurture

I love building those relationships, and I think there's a ton of opportunity for expansion, like working with existing customers, there's just as much, if not more, opportunity than new logos, so I think it's good to figure out a scaled way to where I'll still be in Slack channels with them, and I can fold those insights over, maybe use AI to start, just keep on distilling and kind of gathering all the interactions there, so I can always just have a brief to be able to hop back in, and know exactly, that'll be cool, because like we're starting to do like QBRs, need to do a better job with those, starting to do like listening tours, but there's also like a lot of what you described, and the Edwards Jones example is like, very much like going into the weeds, building the case, and then like surfacing it back up, and then continuing to like find additional use cases, know, but fortunately I gotta run to another meeting, but and this is
Super, super helpful.
I'll regroup with the team.
know we're like, you know, hopefully in the, towards the end of the process here.
So I appreciate you taking the time, Anytime, man.
It was great talking to you again and hope you have a good day.
All right.
You too.
Take care.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Executive interview with Andrew Reese for a Founding Account Executive position.](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=135.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Andrew's strengths lie in customer-centricity, problem-solving, and a holistic approach to sales and go-to-market strategy](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=609.0)
  - [He shared successful deal experiences from Canva ($250K) and Miro ($1.4M), demonstrating his ability to navigate complex sales cycles](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=784.0)
  - The company's current sales cycle is much shorter (often \<1 month) with high-value deals, requiring adaptation to a high-momentum approach
  - [Andrew recognizes the need to adjust his relationship-building strategy to fit the company's faster-paced sales model](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1733.0)

## Topics

### Andrew's Professional Strengths

  - [Customer-centric approach: Focuses on building relationships and problem-solving](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=621.0)
  - [Holistic perspective: Looks beyond sales to understand go-to-market foundations (ICPs, personas, messaging)](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [Process-oriented: Takes a first-principles approach to sales](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=653.0)
  - [Consultative selling: Positions himself as a trusted advisor, becoming an expert on the product and market](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=711.0)

### Successful Deal Example: Edward Jones at Canva

  - [Largest deal of Q2 globally, 5th biggest deal of all time at Canva](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=790.0)
  - [Year-long process, resulting in a $250K deal with plans to grow to $1M ARR](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=804.0)
  - [Approach:](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=809.0)
      - [Talked to 20+ branch office administrators (out of 20,000)](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=882.0)
      - [Developed strong point of view based on collected insights](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=941.0)
      - [Conducted surveys and gathered direct snippets from users](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1020.0)
      - [Formalized a 3-month pilot ($15K) with 300 test users](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1035.0)
      - [Focused on building relationships and aligning on success metrics](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1062.0)
      - [Resulted in overwhelming support and strong case for full implementation](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1103.0)

### Challenging Deal Example: Nubank at Miro

  - [1.4M ARR deal ($700K/year for 2 years)](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1240.0)
  - [Initially started with a 50-license deal, grew to 500-750 users](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1308.0)
  - [Challenges:](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1532.0)
      - [New sales leadership involvement led to misalignment with customer expectations](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
      - [Cultural misunderstanding in sales approach (American vs. Brazilian style)](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1532.0)
  - [Resolution:](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1560.0)
      - [Andrew took ownership, rebuilt internal alignment](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1560.0)
      - [Created a tailored proposal meeting customer's specific requirements](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1588.0)
      - [Successfully closed the deal after addressing relationship concerns](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1620.0)

### Adapting to Current Company's Sales Model

  - Much shorter sales cycles (often \<1 month) for high-value deals
  - [No usage-based growth model, different from previous experiences](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1719.0)
  - [Strategies for adaptation:](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1733.0)
      - [Focus on getting customers into the 60-day opt-out period quickly](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1740.0)
      - [Leverage existing proof points and clearly explain the process](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1751.0)
      - [Maintain relationship-building through scaled methods (e.g., Slack channels, AI-assisted insights gathering)](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1770.0)
      - [Balance high-momentum approach with strategic relationship development](https://fathom.video/share/CKGm6hfsatMF6KeDLZVEyeWffpHugqZ3?tab=summary&timestamp=1802.0)
